label: Problem statement
verbatim: W*_k = arg min_{Wk in k} ||XW - XWk||_F (1); epsilon*_k = ||XW - XW*_k||_F (2)
paraphrase: Activation-aware low-rank compression: minimise Frobenius reconstruction error of XW under rank-k constraint on Wk; epsilon*_k denotes the minimum reconstruction loss.
preconditions: X in R^{l x m} input activations; W in R^{m x n} weight; rank constraint set k = {Wk : rank(Wk)=k}.
consequences: Defines the loss the dynamic-rank allocator uses; the spec's Stage 3 must minimise this exact objective per layer.

label: Compression factorization
verbatim: Wk = AkBk, where Ak in R^{m x k} and Bk in R^{k x n}. The original weight W is subsequently replaced with AkBk.
paraphrase: Compressed layer is stored as two thin factors Ak (m x k) and Bk (k x n).
consequences: Memory drops from m*n to k*(m+n); compression iff k*(m+n)<m*n.

label: KV cache reduction
verbatim: instead of caching output activations XW in R^{l x n}, one can cache intermediate latents X Ak in R^{l x k}, which requires less memory whenever k < n.
paraphrase: By caching X Ak instead of XW, KV-cache memory is reduced when k<n.
consequences: Same factorization can serve weight + KV-cache reduction simultaneously.

label: Theorem 3.1
verbatim: Given input activations X and weight matrix W, let V and Sigma denote the right singular vectors and singular values of Y = XW, respectively. For any k < rank(Y), the optimal solution to the problem defined in (1) and (2) is, W*_k = W Vk Vk^T, ∀ k < rank(Y) (3); epsilon*_k = (sum_{j=k+1}^{rank(Y)} sigma_j^2)^{1/2}, ∀ k < rank(Y) (4)
paraphrase: The optimal activation-aware rank-k compression is W Vk Vk^T where Vk are the top-k right singular vectors of Y=XW; the minimum reconstruction loss is the L2 norm of the tail singular values.
consequences: Closed-form optimal solution; eliminates need for Cholesky/iterative SVD; gives Eq.4 used downstream by Algorithm 2.

label: Eq. 3
verbatim: W*_k = W Vk Vk^T
paraphrase: Closed-form optimal compressed weight: project W onto the span of top-k right singular vectors of Y=XW.
preconditions: Vk obtained from eigen-decomp of Y^T Y.
consequences: Stage 3 of spec must apply Eq.3 (not Eq.5 of SVD-LLM/Cholesky).

label: Eq. 4
verbatim: epsilon*_k = (sum_{j=k+1}^{rank(Y)} sigma_j^2)^{1/2}
paraphrase: Optimal Frobenius reconstruction loss equals L2 norm of truncated tail singular values.
consequences: This is the loss epsilon*_{k_bar,i} fed to Algorithm 2 line 5; spec uses this same formula.

label: Eq. 5
verbatim: X W*_k = X W Vk Vk^T = Y Vk Vk^T = U Sigma (V^T Vk) Vk^T = Uk Sigma_k Vk^T
paraphrase: Algebraic chain showing X W*_k equals the rank-k truncated SVD of Y.
preconditions: U Sigma V^T = SVD(Y); orthonormality V^T Vk = [Ik;0].
consequences: Eckart-Young-Mirsky then guarantees optimality.

label: Eq. 6
verbatim: ||XW - XWk||_F >= ||XW - XW*_k||_F, ∀ Wk ∈ k
paraphrase: W*_k achieves the lower bound for activation-aware Frobenius loss in the rank-k feasible set.
preconditions: Eckart-Young-Mirsky on Y; rank(XWk) <= rank(Wk) = k.
consequences: Optimality of closed-form solution.

label: Eq. 7
verbatim: rank(W*_k) = rank(W Vk Vk^T) <= rank(Vk) = k
paraphrase: Upper bound on rank of closed-form solution.
consequences: Combined with Eq.8 establishes rank(W*_k)=k.

label: Eq. 8
verbatim: rank(W*_k) >= rank(X W*_k) = rank(Uk Sigma_k Vk^T) = k
paraphrase: Lower bound on rank of closed-form solution; uses sigma_k>0 under k<rank(Y).
consequences: Combined with Eq.7 closes feasibility argument: W*_k ∈ k.

label: Eq. 9
verbatim: epsilon*_k = ||XW - X W*_k||_F = ||XW - Uk Sigma_k Vk^T||_F = (sum_{j=k+1}^{rank(Y)} sigma_j^2)^{1/2}
paraphrase: Direct Eckart-Young-Mirsky tail-energy expression for the minimum reconstruction loss.
consequences: Justifies Eq.4.

label: Algorithm 1 (Incremental SVD)
verbatim: Input: X = [x1;...;xl] in R^{l x m}; W in R^{m x n}. Output: Sigma, V of Y=XW. 1: C ← zero matrix of size (n x n) 2: for t=1..l do 3: yt ← xt W 4: C ← C + yt^T yt 5: end for 6: V, Sigma^2, V^T ← eigen-decomposition(C) 7: return Sigma, V
paraphrase: Stream activations through W, accumulate covariance C = sum yt^T yt, then a single eigen-decomposition of C yields Sigma and V of Y=XW.
preconditions: Hooks placed at output of W to capture y_t = x_t W.
consequences: Memory: O(n^2) for C; one EVD; numerically stable; basis for Stage 3 calibration loop.

label: Eq. 10
verbatim: Y^T Y = (U Sigma V^T)^T U Sigma V^T = V Sigma^T U^T U Sigma V^T = V Sigma^2 V^T
paraphrase: Y^T Y = V Sigma^2 V^T, hence eigen-decomp of covariance recovers V and Sigma^2 of Y.
consequences: Avoids forming Y or doing SVD on Y directly.

label: Eq. 11 (effective rank)
verbatim: erank(Sigma) = exp(- sum_{i=1}^{r} p_i ln p_i), where p_i = sigma_i / sum_{j=1}^{r} sigma_j
paraphrase: Effective rank is exponential of Shannon entropy of the normalized spectrum {sigma_i / sum sigma_j}.
preconditions: Singular values of Y=XW (already produced by Algorithm 1).
consequences: Used as quantitative measure of local layer compressibility; lower erank => more compressible.

label: Negative correlation insight
verbatim: Swift-SVD's design of dynamic compression is motivated by the observation that local compressibility (i.e., how well a layer can be approximated at low rank) and end-to-end compressibility (i.e., how much compressing a layer degrades overall model performance) are negatively correlated.
paraphrase: Layers with low effective rank (highly locally compressible) tend to have HIGH layer importance — compressing them aggressively hurts end-to-end performance disproportionately.
preconditions: Effective rank from Eq.11; layer importance per Shi et al. 2025 (ShortGPT codebase).
consequences: Justifies combining beta and epsilon in score si rather than using one alone.

label: Layer importance metric
verbatim: we adopt the standard layer importance metric from prior work (Guo et al., 2025; Shi et al., 2025; Song et al., 2025; Men et al., 2024), which estimates the contribution of the i-th layer to the overall LLM performance. Consequently, lower layer importance implies higher end-to-end compressibility.
paraphrase: beta_i is the standard ShortGPT-style layer importance score; lower beta_i => layer contributes less => safer to compress.
consequences: Spec must compute beta via the ShortGPT recipe (cosine-similarity based block-influence metric).

label: Algorithm 2 (Dynamic Rank Allocation)
verbatim: Input: Frobenius loss epsilon, Layer importance beta, Compression ratio rho, Scaling factor alpha, Retained ratio delta, Size m,n. Output: rank allocation k. 1: beta ← (beta - min(beta))/(max(beta) - min(beta)) + 1   2: k_bar = (m*n)/(m+n) * rho   ▷ Uniform rank   3: for i in {1..L}:   4:   ki ← k_bar * delta   ▷ Guaranteed minimal rank   5:   si ← (beta_i)^alpha * (log(e + epsilon_{k_bar,i}))^(1-alpha)   6: end for   7: b ← k_bar*L - sum_i ki   ▷ Flexible rank pool   8: for i in {1..L}:   9:   ki ← ki + [b * (si / sum_j sj)]   10: end for   11: return k
paraphrase: 1) Min-max normalize beta into [0,1] then shift to [1,2]; 2) compute uniform rank k_bar = m*n/(m+n)*rho; 3) seed each layer with floor rank k_bar*delta; 4) compute compressibility score si = (beta_i)^alpha * (log(e + epsilon*_{k_bar,i}))^(1-alpha); 5) distribute the leftover rank pool b proportionally to si.
preconditions: epsilon*_{k_bar,i} from Eq.4 at uniform rank k_bar; beta_i ShortGPT score; alpha in [0,1]; delta in (0,1].
consequences: Determines per-layer rank ki; spec's Stage 3 redistribution must implement this exact rule.

label: Eq. 12 (compressibility score)
verbatim: si = (beta_i)^alpha * (log(e + epsilon*_{k_bar,i}))^(1-alpha)
paraphrase: Compressibility score combines normalized layer importance beta_i (in [1,2]) and log of minimum reconstruction loss at uniform rank, weighted by alpha.
consequences: alpha=0 => loss-driven only; alpha=1 => importance-driven only; scoring drives the rank pool b distribution.

label: Beta normalization rule
verbatim: beta ← (beta - min(beta))/(max(beta) - min(beta)) + 1
paraphrase: Layer importance beta is min-max normalized into [0,1] and then shifted by +1 so the final range is [1,2].
preconditions: Raw layer importances beta_i from ShortGPT-style recipe.
consequences: Ensures (beta_i)^alpha is in [1,2^alpha], avoiding zero-collapse when alpha->0.

label: Uniform rank k_bar
verbatim: k_bar = (m*n)/(m+n) * rho
paraphrase: Per-layer uniform rank that hits a target compression ratio rho on a single (m x n) weight.
consequences: Used both as the floor (k_bar*delta) and as the operating point at which epsilon*_{k_bar,i} is computed for scoring.

label: Guaranteed minimal rank
verbatim: ki ← k_bar * delta   ▷ Guaranteed minimal rank
paraphrase: Each layer is seeded with at least k_bar*delta rank before any redistribution.
consequences: Acts as 'preserved ratio' floor — prevents over-compression of any layer.

label: Flexible rank pool b
verbatim: b ← k_bar * L - sum_{i=1}^{L} ki
paraphrase: Leftover budget b = total uniform budget minus already-assigned floor; redistributed proportionally to si.
preconditions: Floors ki = k_bar*delta assigned for all L layers.
consequences: Ensures total rank budget == k_bar*L (i.e. matches uniform total).

label: Rank redistribution rule
verbatim: ki ← ki + [b * (si / sum_{j=1}^{L} sj)]
paraphrase: Distribute the flexible pool b proportionally to each layer's compressibility score si (rounded with [.]).
consequences: Defines exactly how non-uniform ranks are produced from (beta, epsilon, alpha, delta, rho).

label: alpha grid (paper-exact)
verbatim: Swift-SVD uses a fixed retention ratio delta = 0.5 and 11 scaling factors alpha = [0, 0.1, 0.2, ..., 1] to generate 11 candidate rank allocations.
paraphrase: Default hyperparameters: delta = 0.5; alpha grid = {0, 0.1, 0.2, ..., 0.9, 1.0} (11 candidates).
consequences: Spec must search exactly these 11 alpha values per the paper-exact protocol.

label: Validation-set alpha search procedure
verbatim: Swift-SVD first generates candidate rank allocations based on layer importance and local compression loss, then selects the best one via lightweight validation: each candidate is used to compress the model using the optimal solution in (3), and the resulting models are evaluated on a validation set to identify the one that yields the best end-to-end performance. Thanks to the closed-form spectral solution, this grid search requires no retraining and is easily parallelized.
paraphrase: alpha is selected by exhaustive grid search: for each of the 11 alpha values, build the compressed model with Eq.3 and evaluate on a validation set; pick the alpha minimizing/maximizing the validation metric.
preconditions: Closed-form Eq.3 enables fast re-compression per alpha; alpha grid of 11 values.
consequences: This is the canonical 'paper-exact WikiText-2 PPL validation' alpha selection cited by the spec; no retraining.

label: Alpha endpoints semantics
verbatim: alpha = 0 reduces the strategy to a purely loss-driven allocation, whereas alpha = 1 prioritizes layer importance exclusively.
paraphrase: alpha controls the trade-off: 0 = score depends only on Frobenius loss; 1 = score depends only on layer importance.
consequences: Sanity check on the search range; both endpoints are sub-optimal in practice.

label: Delta as performance stabilizer
verbatim: delta acts as a performance stabilizer. Without a preserved ratio delta = 0, unrestricted dynamic allocation can inadvertently destroy the representational capacity of critical layers, leading to significant degradation. ... when delta = 1.0, the rank allocation k matches the uniform target rank k_bar for all modules, rendering the strategy equivalent to uniform compression.
paraphrase: delta=0 yields unstable over-compression; delta=1 reduces to uniform; delta=0.5 is the recommended default.
consequences: Justifies fixed default delta=0.5 in the paper-exact protocol.

label: Per-projection uniformity (no per-projection bias)
verbatim: Swift-SVD can be seamlessly applied to all types of weight matrices–such as query, key, value, and others–in the same manner. For simplicity, we use the generic notation W throughout our exposition.
paraphrase: The same algorithm is applied independently per projection (Q,K,V,O,Gate,Up,Down) without explicit per-projection rank bias inside Swift-SVD itself.
preconditions: Each W is treated as one (m x n) matrix with its own X, Y, V, Sigma.
consequences: Any per-projection rank bias (e.g. Stage 3 D7a) is NOT sanctioned by Swift-SVD itself; would be a deviation.

label: Per-(layer,projection) scoring granularity
verbatim: Figure 3. Layer-wise NER across distinct modules and layer importance in Mistral-7B with dataset C4. (modules: query key value output gate up down)
paraphrase: The paper computes effective rank / Frobenius loss per (layer, projection) pair (q,k,v,o,gate,up,down for LLaMA/Mistral; q,k,v,o,fc1,fc2 for OPT/LLaMA2 reported), but redistribution in Algorithm 2 is per layer i in {1..L} (not per projection).
preconditions: Algorithm 2 indexed by layer i only.
consequences: Granularity of allocation = layer; per-projection compressibility is reported but the rank pool is not split per projection in the paper's prescription.

label: Calibration sample size N=256
verbatim: we adopt the standard setting of N = 256 to ensure a fair comparison with baselines.
paraphrase: Default calibration sample count is 256.
preconditions: WikiText-2 / C4 with seq_len=2048 per sample; Alpaca/zero-shot use raw entries with formatting templates.
consequences: Spec must use 256 samples for calibration (or document deviation).

label: Sequence length 2048
verbatim: For text datasets such as WikiText-2 and C4, each sample is processed with a fixed sequence length of 2048 tokens.
paraphrase: Each WikiText-2 / C4 calibration sample uses fixed 2048 tokens.
consequences: Locks calibration token-budget at N*seq_len = 256 * 2048 = 524288 tokens.

label: Conversational/zero-shot calibration formatting
verbatim: for conversational datasets like Alpaca and zero-shot common sense reasoning benchmarks (e.g., PIQA), samples are constructed by formatting individual raw data entries according to their official prompt templates and concatenating them as discrete, individually separated instances.
paraphrase: For Alpaca/PIQA/etc., each calibration sample is one prompt-formatted entry; sequence length is variable.
preconditions: Official prompt templates available per dataset.
consequences: Variable-length calibration may break Cholesky-based methods (X^T X may be non-PD); Swift-SVD remains stable.

label: Hardware/software stack
verbatim: All experiments are conducted on machines with 2× NVIDIA 5090 GPUs (32GB each), though all evaluations are executed on a single GPU without distributed computation. We use PyTorch 2.8.0 and Hugging Face Transformers 4.57.3 for model loading, compression, and inference. All evaluations are conducted in inference mode without gradient computation.
paraphrase: Single-GPU eval on RTX 5090 (32GB); PyTorch 2.8.0; Transformers 4.57.3; inference mode only.
consequences: No training/gradients during compression.

label: Models and datasets
verbatim: we experiment on a diverse suite of LLMs: LLaMA-7B, LLaMA2-7B, OPT-6.7B, Mistral-7B, and Qwen3 (4B, 8B). We assess performance using nine standard benchmarks: WikiText2, C4, and Alpaca for language modeling (perplexity), and OpenBookQA, WinoGrande, HellaSwag, ARC-Easy, PIQA, and MathQA for zero-shot common sense reasoning.
paraphrase: Six LLMs; nine eval benchmarks (3 PPL, 6 zero-shot QA).
consequences: Defines the validation surface used by the alpha grid search (paper uses C4 calibration in Table 1).

label: Evaluation metrics
verbatim: We evaluate model performance using perplexity (PPL) and zero-shot accuracy, computational efficiency via compression time (in seconds), inference efficiency through memory usage (in GB) and throughput (in tokens/second), and numerical stability by reconstruction loss, computed from (2).
paraphrase: Metrics: PPL, zero-shot acc, compression time, peak/weight memory, throughput, reconstruction loss epsilon*_k.
consequences: Numerical stability is benchmarked against the theoretical minimum given by Eq.4.

label: Table 1 LLaMA-7B ratio 0.8
verbatim: 0.8 (10.1 GB) Swift-SVD: WikiText-2 7.91 C4 11.42 Avg 0.50; Swift-SVD*: WikiText-2 7.84 C4 11.15 Avg 0.51
paraphrase: Ratio 0.8: uniform Swift-SVD 7.91/11.42 PPL; dynamic Swift-SVD* 7.84/11.15 PPL; mem 10.1 GB.
consequences: Dynamic allocation yields ~0.07 WikiText-2 / 0.27 C4 PPL improvement at ratio 0.8.

label: Table 1 LLaMA-7B ratio 0.6
verbatim: 0.6 (7.7 GB) Swift-SVD: WikiText-2 13.42 C4 23.32 Avg 0.43; Swift-SVD*: WikiText-2 13.29 C4 21.92 Avg 0.44
paraphrase: Ratio 0.6: uniform 13.42/23.32; dynamic 13.29/21.92.
consequences: n/a

label: Table 1 LLaMA-7B ratio 0.4
verbatim: 0.4 (5.3 GB) Swift-SVD: WikiText-2 64.16 C4 143.74 Avg 0.34; Swift-SVD*: WikiText-2 62.32 C4 137.77 Avg 0.34
paraphrase: Ratio 0.4 (aggressive): uniform 64.16/143.74; dynamic 62.32/137.77.
consequences: Dynamic alloc helps even under heavy compression.

label: Table 1 baseline LLaMA-7B
verbatim: 1.0 (12.6 GB) Baseline: WikiText-2 5.68 C4 7.34 Avg 0.57
paraphrase: Uncompressed LLaMA-7B PPL ref: 5.68 WikiText-2, 7.34 C4, 0.57 avg-acc.
consequences: Reference for relative degradation.

label: Table 2 cross-model 0.8
paraphrase: Cross-architecture results at ratio 0.8: Swift-SVD* outperforms uniform on all three models.
consequences: Dynamic allocation generalises across architectures.

label: Table 3 cross-domain calib
verbatim: Calibration on C4 then evaluation: ratio 0.8 — C4 11.34/11.42; WikiText-2 7.86 (calibrated)/11.33 (C4-calib); Alpaca 8.49/10.51 (256 samples)
paraphrase: Cross-domain PPL drop: when calibrated on C4 only, WikiText-2 PPL inflates ~3.5 points at ratio 0.8 — confirms activation-aware behaviour.
consequences: Calibration-domain drift causes meaningful PPL degradation.

label: Table 4 end-to-end speedup
verbatim: 256 samples 0.8: Dobi-SVD(w/o) 30,862s 1.0×; SVD-LLM(W) 1,080s 19.8×; Swift-SVD 540s 76.9×.
paraphrase: At 256 calibration samples, Swift-SVD is 76.9× faster than Dobi-SVD baseline and ~2× faster than SVD-LLM(W).
preconditions: Compression of full model at ratio 0.8.
consequences: Closed-form spectral solution drives speedup.

label: Table 5 numerical stability
paraphrase: Swift-SVD attains the theoretical minimum reconstruction loss exactly across all matrix sizes; SVD-LLM (Cholesky) and Dobi-SVD (Incremental PCA) drift by ~0.03–4% above optimum.
consequences: Justifies Swift-SVD's numerical-stability advantage; spec should preserve FP32 covariance accumulation to retain this property.

label: Table 6 ablation (alpha/delta variants)
verbatim: C4 ratio 0.8: Swift-SVD 11.42; Swift-SVD(C) 16.04; Swift-SVD(I) 14.88; Swift-SVD†(C) 11.78; Swift-SVD†(I) 11.73; Swift-SVD* 11.15.
paraphrase: Ablation: pure Frobenius (C) or pure importance (I) without delta floor is WORSE than uniform; adding delta=0.5 floor recovers; full grid (Swift-SVD*) is best.
consequences: Confirms necessity of (a) delta floor and (b) joint alpha-grid search; either alone is insufficient.

label: Variant definitions for ablation
verbatim: Swift-SVD, a baseline that assigns a uniform rank; (2) Swift-SVD(C), which mirrors the dynamic rank allocation strategy of SVD-LLM v2 by allocating ranks solely based on Frobenius loss, and Swift-SVD(I), which allocate ranks based on layer importance without any preserved ratio; and (3) Swift-SVD†(C) and Swift-SVD†(I), which incorporate a fixed preserved ratio of delta = 0.5.
paraphrase: Variant taxonomy: uniform; (C) Frobenius-only no-floor; (I) importance-only no-floor; †(C) and †(I) add delta=0.5 floor; * is full Algorithm 2 + alpha grid search.
consequences: Ablation isolates contribution of (a) per-layer signal choice and (b) preserved-ratio floor.

label: log(e + epsilon) damping rationale
verbatim: si = (beta_i)^alpha * (log(e + epsilon*_{k_bar,i}))^(1-alpha)
paraphrase: The minimum reconstruction loss epsilon*_{k_bar,i} is wrapped in log(e + .) — this damps large-magnitude losses (which can span orders of magnitude across layers/projections) into a similar dynamic range as beta in [1,2].
consequences: Avoids loss term dominating the score solely because some layers have huge spectral norms (cf. §B.2 'extreme unevenness').

label: Frobenius-only allocation bias
verbatim: the singular value distribution of both Key and Value modules exhibits a pronounced spectral disparity. Even on a logarithmic scale, we observe a massive magnitude gap... Crucially, this extreme unevenness renders rank allocation methods based solely on Frobenius norm minimization ineffective. Since the Frobenius loss is disproportionately sensitive to large singular values, such methods suffer from severe numerical bias, often assigning insufficient ranks to layers with smaller spectral norms.
paraphrase: Pure Frobenius-loss allocation is biased toward layers with large singular values; layers with smaller spectral norms get too few ranks. This motivates blending with beta and the log-damping in Eq.12.
consequences: Spec must NOT use pure-Frobenius redistribution (would replicate Swift-SVD(C) failure mode).

label: Calibration size sensitivity
verbatim: performance improves rapidly in the low-sample regime but shows diminishing returns as N increases further. While larger samples continue to yield marginal gains, we adopt the standard setting of N = 256 to ensure a fair comparison with baselines.
paraphrase: Performance saturates beyond ~256 samples; diminishing returns thereafter.
consequences: 256 is sufficient; spec can lock at 256 without sacrificing quality.

label: Cross-domain calibration validity
verbatim: We utilized the C4 dataset for calibration and evaluated the compressed model across three distinct domains. As illustrated in Table 3, significant PPL degradation is observed when the calibrated model with C4 is applied to WikiText-2 or Alpaca, demonstrating that Swift-SVD is acutely activation-aware.
paraphrase: Calibrating on C4 then evaluating on WikiText-2/Alpaca produces meaningful PPL degradation, confirming Swift-SVD captures input-distribution-specific covariance.
consequences: If the spec's Stage 3 alpha-search uses WikiText-2 PPL, the model should also be CALIBRATED on WikiText-2 (or accept ~3-pt PPL inflation as paper-faithful for C4 calib).

label: Headline contributions
verbatim: (1) optimal solution for activation-aware low-rank compression of LLMs that requires only a single eigenvalue decomposition; (2) fast layer-wise compression using the closed-form formulation, facilitating grid search for optimal dynamic rank allocation beyond uniform compression; (3) a negative correlation between layer importance and compression loss, providing insight into the design of dynamic compression strategies.
paraphrase: Four contributions: (i) closed-form one-EVD optimum; (ii) grid-search-enabled dynamic allocation; (iii) negative-correlation insight; (iv) extensive experiments.
consequences: Frames what the spec must reproduce vs may safely defer.

label: Numerical stability advantage
verbatim: Swift-SVD is not only fast (see Section 4.2 for experimental results), but also exhibits strong numerical stability (see Section 4.3 for experimental results).
paraphrase: Single eigen-decomposition of the n x n covariance avoids Cholesky and iterated SVD, eliminating both their failure modes.
consequences: Spec must compute C in FP32 to retain stability; downcasting may invalidate this claim.

label: Visual confirmation of negative correlation
verbatim: Figure 3 visualizes the normalized effective rank and normalized layer importance across all seven weight matrices in a representative LLM. Strikingly, the two exhibit a clear negative correlation: layers with high importance tend to have lower effective rank.
paraphrase: Negative correlation between effective rank and layer importance is visually confirmed across q,k,v,o,gate,up,down for Mistral-7B; further models in §B.4.
consequences: Empirical foundation for Eq.12 mixed scoring.

label: Closed-form solution span
verbatim: Swift-SVD, an activation-aware, closed-form compression framework that simultaneously guarantees theoretical optimum, practical efficiency and numerical stability.
paraphrase: Single framework reaching optimum + speed + stability — distinguishing claim vs predecessors (FWSVD, ASVD, SVD-LLM, SVD-LLM v2, Dobi-SVD).
consequences: Establishes the deviation surface: any spec choice that breaks one of {optimum, speed, stability} is a deviation.

label: Numerical stability evaluated in FP32
verbatim: Reconstruction loss and absolute error for randomly generated matrices of varying shapes under ratio of 0.6 (FP32).
paraphrase: Numerical-stability experiment uses FP32.
consequences: Paper does not certify Swift-SVD's exact-optimum claim under BF16/FP16; downcasting at compress-time is a deviation surface.

label: U-shaped PPL vs alpha
verbatim: Figure A.1. Impact of hyperparameters alpha and delta on model performance for dynamic compression. Both configurations exhibit a U-shaped PPL curve.
paraphrase: PPL as a function of alpha is U-shaped with optimum in the interior of [0,1] (e.g. ~0.5); endpoints (alpha=0 or 1) are the worst.
consequences: Justifies that grid search is essential — neither pure-loss nor pure-importance is optimal.

label: Optimal grid PPL = 11.15
verbatim: Optimal: 11.15
paraphrase: Best PPL across the alpha grid (delta=0.5, ratio 0.8, LLaMA-7B, C4) is 11.15 — matches Swift-SVD* in Table 1.
consequences: Reproducibility check: Stage 3's grid search must yield ~11.15 C4 PPL on LLaMA-7B at ratio 0.8.

label: Calibration-data-task hierarchy
verbatim: We observe a clear hierarchy: Each ≳ All > C4. Domain Specificity (Each): Achieves the highest accuracy... Robust Aggregation (All): Matches the oracle Each performance closely... Generalization Limit (C4): The notable accuracy drop illustrates the inadequacy of general-purpose data...
paraphrase: Task-specific or pooled-task calibration outperforms generic C4; gap can be 0.05+ avg acc at ratio 0.4.
consequences: Spec's downstream task accuracy depends on what calibration data is used.

label: delta=0.5 default rationale
verbatim: Swift-SVD uses a fixed retention ratio delta = 0.5 ... delta acts as a performance stabilizer ... when delta = 1.0, the rank allocation k matches the uniform target rank k_bar for all modules, rendering the strategy equivalent to uniform compression.
paraphrase: delta=0.5 is the canonical, ablation-justified preserved-ratio floor; delta=1 collapses to uniform; delta=0 collapses to unrestricted (unstable).
consequences: Spec's Stage 3 should use delta=0.5 unless explicitly sanctioned otherwise.
